review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions on cluster bandwidth hierarchy, topology-aware thinking, and the systems framing of distributed training. No separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: As communication moves farther out in the cluster hierarchy, what trend matters most for distributed training design?
choices: Bandwidth increases with distance, so distant GPUs are easiest to coordinate
Bandwidth stays roughly constant, so topology barely matters
Bandwidth drops with distance, so heavy communication should stay local when possible
Only latency matters; bandwidth is irrelevant
question_bank.explanation: Communication becomes more constrained as devices are farther apart, so algorithms should place the heaviest traffic on the fastest local links.

transcript_quotes: and imagine these in the context of a full data center.

question_bank.question: Why is it useful to think of the whole GPU cluster as one giant computer?
choices: Because each GPU already stores the entire training dataset
Because cluster-scale training is really about placing computation and communication across a hierarchy of connected resources
Because it eliminates the need for batching
Because it guarantees every link has the same bandwidth
question_bank.explanation: That viewpoint helps you reason about the full system: compute placement, communication paths, and bandwidth limits across many levels of hardware.

question_bank.question: What is the core systems problem of distributed training, as framed here?
choices: Choosing the best tokenizer before training starts
Splitting computation and overlapping it with communication across the hierarchy
Eliminating all communication by duplicating every activation
Ensuring every GPU sees the same minibatch
question_bank.explanation: Distributed training succeeds when work is partitioned well and communication is hidden or reduced so compute devices stay busy.

transcript_quotes: to be split up your computation.
